text: ERROL, a parish and v. in Perthshire, 9 m. E of Perth. Pop. of p. in 1801, 2,653; in 1841, 2,832, of whom 1,147 were in the v.

ERROMANGO, or Koromango, an island in the S. Pacific ocean, one of the New Hebrides, in S lat. 18° 48', E long. 169° 20', 20 m. N of Tanna, discovered by Cook in 1774. It is about 90 m. in circumf. The land is very high to the E, and is indented on that side by a deep bay with a low shore. In Dillon's bay, on the SW side of the island, there is anchorage in 21 fath., but on an abrupt bottom. Mr. Bennet observed madrepores embedded in the solid rock at an alt. of 500 ft. above sea-level on this island. The inhabitants are black, of the middle size, and well-shaped. They live in houses about 5 ft. high, and from 10 to 20 ft. in length, covered with thatch, and their plantations are regularly laid out and fenced round. Their weapons are clubs about 3 ft. in length, bows and arrows of small size, rude spears, and slings. When visited by the late lamented missionary, Williams, on the 29th of Nov., 1839, the natives of this island declined all inter- ~ course, and as they spoke a language quite different from that of the windward islands, it was impossible to converse with them. The undaunted missionary however landed, and accompanied a party of the natives to a little distance from the shore, when, without any provocation, he and his three companions were attacked and despatched with clubs. Their bodies,—or rather a few relics of them,—were afterwards recovered by peaceful negociation, and conveyed to the Navigators' islands, where they received Christian burial on the 31st March, 1840.

ERRONAN, or FUTUNA, an island of the New Hebrides, in the S. Pacific ocean, in S lat. 19° 31', E long. 170° 2', about 15 m. in circumf. It is elevated, and has its summit flattened somewhat like the Table mountain of the Cape.
ERRUGUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 8 m. NE of Coimbatur.
ERRUR, a fortified town of the S of India, in the prov. of Mysore, district of Malabar, in N lat. 13° 48'.
ERSA, a port of France, on the N coast of the island of Corsica, near Cape Corse. Pop. 846. It had, in 1841, 16 small vessels of an aggregate tonnage of 105 tons.
ERSCHHAUSEN, a village of Prussian Westphalia, in the bail. of Bischoffstein.
ERSE, a river of Hanover, which runs into the Fuhse 8 m. S of Zell.
ERSEK-UJOUR, a town of Hungary, in the com. and 22 m. SE of Neutra. Pop. 6,785.
ERSINGEN, a village of Germany, in the duchy of Baden, with 1,000 inhabitants, 9 m. SE of Durlach.
ERSKINE, a parish in the co. of Renfrew, bounded by the river Clyde on the N, 5 m. WNW of Renfrew. Area 6,365 acres. Pop. in 1801, 847; in 1841, 1,407.

ERSTEIN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, on the l. bank of the Ill, 12 m. SSW of Strasburg. Pop. 3,357. It has bleachfields and several small manufactories of tobacco, cordage, and earthenware.
ERTH (SAINT), a parish of Cornwall, 3 m. NE of Marazion. Area 3,050 acres. Pop. 2,452.
ERTINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, near the Danube, 14 m. W of Biberach. Pop. 1,911 in 1840. It has considerable linen manufactories.
ERTVAAG-OE, an island off the W coast of Norway, in N lat. 63° 13′, 40 m. NE of Christiansand. It is 12 m. in length from W to E, and about 9 m. in breadth.
ERTVELDE, a commune and town of Belgium, in E. Flanders, 3 m. S of Sas-de-Gand. Pop. 3,135.
ERTZOG, a town of Russian Georgia, in the district and 27 m. W of Telav.
ERUMBACH, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of Starkenburg, on the Gersprenz. Pop. 1,217.
ERVAN (SAINT), a parish of Cornwall, 4 m. SSW of Padstow. Area 3,110 acres. Pop. 477.
ERVEDOSA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 16 m. ESE of Lamego, on the l. bank of the Duero. Pop. 1,000.

ERVY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aube, arrond. of Troyes.—The cant., comprising 14 com., had a pop. of 11,284 in 1841.— The com., 20 m. SSW of Troyes, on the r, bank of the Armance, had a pop. of 1,821. It has manufactories of coarse linens.
ERWARTON, a parish in Suffolk, 7 m. SE of Ipswich. Area 870 acres. Pop. 199.
ERWASH, a river of England, which rises in the co. of Nottingham, and falls into the Trent 4 m. SW of Nottingham.
ERWETEGEM, a commune of Belgium, in the remarkable, and for size only. There are 2 Greek III. PRICE CONTRACTOR ERZ prov. of E. Flanders, arrond. and 10 m. E of Oudenarde. Pop. 1,891.
ERWITE, a small town of Prussian Westphalia, 5 m. S of Lippstadt. Pop. 650.
ERWYRYS, a township of Denbighshire, 4 m. E of Ruthin, in the p. of Llanarmon. Pop. 681.
ERYX, or Santo Juliano, a mountain of Sicily, to the E of Trapani. Alt. 2,175 ft. See Trapani.
ERZBERG. See EISENARZ.
ERZEN, a village of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. and 80 m. ENE of Diarbekir, on a small lake which discharges itself into the Tigris.

ERZENGA'N, or Erzinga'n, a town and sanjak of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Erzerum.—The town, 30 m. S by W from Karghan, is situated on the W bank of the Euphrates, at the confluence of the Kail, at the W end of a beautiful plain, 20 m. in length, and from 7 to 9 m. broad, skirted by the Gujik mountains on the S. The town contains about 3,000 houses. The plain of E. has nearly 100 villages or hamlets scattered over its surface. It is fertile in wheat and in fruit, and has several tracts of good pasture.

ERZERUM, or ARZERUM, a pashalic of Asiatic Turkey, lying between the parallels of 39° and 41°, and the meridians of 39° and 44°. It is bounded on the N by the pash. of Trebisond, from which it is chiefly separated by the Tekres, Kuttagh, Messchidi, and Antza mountains, forming a nearly continuous chain, running SW by W, and NE by E; on the NE by the pash. of Akhalzike; on the E by the pash. of Kars, Russian Armenia, and the pash. of Bayazid; on the S by the pash. of Van, from which it is separated by the Arghi-Seiban-Dagh, and the upper course of the Murad-shai; and by the pash. of Diarbekir, from which it is separated by the Kashmir- Dagh; and on the W by the pash. of Sivas. It is a lofty table-land, in which the Araxes flowing to the E, the twin head-streams of the Euphrates flowing to the W, and the Tchorak flowing N to the Black sea, have their sources. This pash, has recently had the pash. of Kars and of Bayazid added to it; and in rank and extent only yields to that of Bagdad. It is thinly peopled, but contains numerous highly fertile districts which yield grain of a peculiarly fine quality. The mountain-ranges are chiefly peopled by Kurds, who pay little or no tribute to the sultan.

ERZERUM, or ARZRUM, the chief city of Armenia, and also the cap. of the above pash., situated 250 m. NNE of Aleppo, in N lat. 39° 57', E long. 41° 20' [Kinneir]; N lat. 39° 58', E long. 41° 36' [Beauchamp]; N lat. 39° 55′ 20″, E long. 41° 18′ 30″ [ Glascott]; 24 hours from Erzengan, 48 from Mush, and 36 from Kars, on a rising ground at the base of a chain of mountains which are commonly covered with snow. Alt. above sea-level 6,225 ft. [Brant.] The streets are in general paved, and the houses are built of a dark coloured stone, with rafters of wood, and terraces. Unlike the cities of Persia, the houses of E. open upon the street, but the windows are guarded by iron gratings, and wooden lattices secure privacy. E. is built upon a rock, and is protected by a citadel, which is a strong massive fortress, surrounded by a double wall of stone, and has 4 gates covered with plates of iron. The pasha and the greater number of the inhabitants reside here. This fortress, however, is commanded by the Tap-dagh, a hill in the neighbourhood. The city itself is surrounded by a triple wall of stone. In the eastern part of the city there is an old brick tower, the loftiest building in the place, with a clock on the summit, which strikes the hours with tolerable regularity. Mosques are numerous, amounting, according to some accounts, to 200; but only one of them is at all Armenian convents. Among the most remarkable buildings of E. are an ancient Grecian monastery, and an old Armenian church. Both these buildings, and especially the cloister, bear evident marks of former splendour and importance; the former is now used by the Turks as an arsenal, and the latter as a foundry. In some cells of the convent the Russian officers engaged in the late campaign discovered a number of ancient shields, helmets, bows, arrows, and swords, which latter bore a strong resemblance to the short broad-swords used by the Russian cuirassiers. The shields were large and square, made of wood, covered with leather, and varnished, which has well-preserved their exceedingly brilliant colours. Of the helmets, one was entirely overlaid with gold, exquisitely wrought; and nearly all the remainder ornamented with Syriac inscriptions in silver. They are all of regular forms, and appear to have been cast; some are plain, and others richly chased. These helmets belonged, without doubt, to Arabians of the period of the government of the caliphs. The marketplaces are spacious, and well-supplied with provisions; and fruit is brought from a distance of 3 or 4 days' journey. Manufactures of considerable extent are established here; and an extensive trade is carried on in the transit to Constantinople of articles from Persia, and from the countries to the NW of Hindostan. The transit trade of E. has considerably increased since the establishment of weekly steam-boats on the Black sea. The caravans from Tiflis to E. accomplish the journey in 15 days. From the East are brought Cashmere shawls, silk, cotton, tobacco, rice, indigo, madder, and drugs; from the West, broad cloth, chintzes, shawls, and cutlery. Little is seen, however, of these wares, except at the custom-house and in the khans.—The pop. is estimated at 15,000; it formerly amounted to 100,000 or 130,000, Turks, Greeks, Persians, and Armenians, and at the time of the Russian invasion E. contained 11,733 Turkish and 4,645 Christian houses, or a pop. of about 80,000 [Smith]; but when the Russian army, which had wintered at E. in 1829, evacuated Turkish Armenia, a vast number of the Christian inhabitants accompanied them.—From the purity of the air the inhabitants are stout and healthy; but the cold during winter, which commences in August, is intense, and snow remains on the ground from October until March. The plain, at one extremity of which, on the SE side, the city stands, is more than 6,000 ft. above sea-level, and is surrounded on every side by high mountains. It stretches W from the Kebbun range, and is between 30 and 40 m. in length, and from 15 to 20 m. in breadth, and is watered by the Kara-su or W branch of the Euphrates. Its surface is undulating and dry towards the W; but the opposite and lower extremity is occupied by marshes. The following table of temp. at E. is from the observations of Mr. Consul Brant: December, The soil in the neighbourhood of E. produces rye and Indian corn, but is deficient in garden-produce ERZ churches, and 1 Armenian, and near the city are 4; and in fire-wood. The surrounding country abounds in metals and ores. History.] At the beginning of the 5th cent, the emperor, Theodosius the younger, ordered a city and fortress to be built, which might, in case of necessity, afford the inhabitants of that part of Armenia, and the Greek army, protection against the attacks of the Persians; and the Patrician, Anatolius, was commissioned to select a site according with the views of the emperor. Anatolius fixed upon a spot which had from time immemorial been called by the Armenians Karin or Garin; and there built a city, to which the Armenians gave the name of Karnu-Kakakh, that is 'the city of Karin;' but the Greeks called it after its founder, Theodosiopolis. Karin is described by native historians as being situated in a plain which forms a circuit of several leagues in extent, and is extremely beautiful and fertile. In this plain, at the foot of a picturesque hill, whence many refreshing springs flow, the new city was built, and surrounded by a deep trench and a rampart, furnished with watch towers. When, at an after period, the Greeks and the Persians shared Armenia between them, Theodosiopolis remained in the hands of the Greeks. The Persian king, Kavad I., obtained possession of the city in 502, by the treachery of the governor Constantius; but before one year had passed over, it again fell into the hands of the Greeks. Towards the end of the 6th cent., Karin was once more captured by the Persians, and a great part of the pop. removed to Hamadan, a city in Persian Irak. In 647 the city was pillaged by the Arabs; and more than a century elapsed before the Greeks regained possession of it, under the emperor Constantine Copronymus, in 755. Constantine caused the ramparts to be demolished, and removed the Mussulman inhabitants, together with their property, to the Grecian provinces. Little time, however, elapsed before it was rebuilt and inhabited by the Arabs. The Greeks took it more than once by storm in the course of the following cents., but were not able to maintain themselves long in this quarter. It is probable that Erzerum-a contraction of Arze-el-Rúm, the Arabic name of the city-became common about this time, the Arabs designating the adjacent territory 'the Land of the Rums,' [i, e., of the Greeks,] to distinguish it from the city of Arzan which stood not far to the E, and because it long formed the frontier position between the Arabian possessions and the Greek provinces of Asia. The Armenians, however, appear to have adhered to the native and ancient appellation of the place; for, in the 13th cent., their historian Cyriacos notices two Armenian noblemen as tributaries of the sultan of the 'city Karin,'-probably meaning the sultan of Iconieme, who held the city in the first half of the 13th cent. In 1247, the Monguls appeared before E., and summoned the inhabitants to surrender at discretion. On their refusal it was taken by storm, and delivered up to plunder. Upon this occasion a great number of MSS., it is alleged, were destroyed by the Monguls, who at last set fire to the town, and thousands of Mahommedans and Christians perished by the conflagration. Some time after, E. was rebuilt by order of the Monguls, who, according to their well-known system of tolerance, placed in it a bishop named Sarkis. This bishop completed the restoration of the town and re-assembled its scattered inhabitants. After the dissolution of the Mongul dominion, the town and fortress came into the possession of the Turks; and peace was concluded here between the Porte and Persia in 1735. The Russians took E. by capitulation in the beginning of July 1829.

ERZGEBIRGE, or ORE MOUNTAINS, an extensive mountain-range in Germany, extending from the Elbe in a WSW direction to the sources of the White Elster, a distance of nearly 100 m. Throughout a considerable part of its course, this range separates Bohemia from Saxony. It is of primitive formation; and, as its name implies, is singularly rich in metals, while its higher regions are covered with dense forests which supply fuel to the innumerable smelting works scattered over the chain. The highest part of the range occurs in about 13° E long. In this quarter the Kerlberg attains an alt. of 4,212 villages. The Lutheran creed is everywhere predoforming the declivity of the chain of which it bears and the Katzenstein. The principal rivers are the two Muldawas, the Pleisse, and the Pöhle. The climate is rigorous in the mountains, and the inhabitants can seldom dispense with fires, but the air is pure and healthy. Agriculture is well-conducted, but has to contend with climate; rye and oats prosper better than wheat and barley; and the circle does not nearly produce corn sufficient for its consumption. Wood abounds; but the consumption is so enormous, on account of the many smelting furnaces, that the extent of forests is rapidly diminishing. Besides the manufactures occasioned by mining, there are many others conducted in the mountains, such as that of linen, worsted, cotton cloth, ribbons, and lace, which is one of the principal employments of the women as well as embroidery. The commerce of the circle is important. The great manufacturers go with their merchandise to the German fairs; but as there is no navigable river, the expense of transportation is considerable. The circle is governed in the same manner as the others, but the miners have their own jurisdiction and administration.—Freiberg is the principal town of this circle. It lies 1,179 ft. above the sea; and is situated on a branch of the Muldawa, near the mountains which separate Saxony from Bohemia. Its other towns are Altenburg, Chemnitz, and Zwickau. The circle is divided into 17 bailiwicks; and comprises the grafschaft of Salm-Wildenfels.
ERZINGAN, a parish and village of Baden, in the Upper Rhine circle, bail. of Jestetten. Pop. 734.
ERZINGAN. See ERZENGAN.
ERZO, a town of Georgia, in the prov. of Kakheti, 15 m. SW of Kakheti.
ESALAY, a large walled town of Katunga, in W Africa, 6 m. from Shea.
ESARO, a small river of Naples, in Calabria- Citra, which rises in Monte Isauro, and joins the Coscile, on the r. bank.
ESBARRES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, cant. of Saint-Jean-de- Losne. Pop. 1,123.
ESCAILLON, a river of Belgium, which runs into the Scheldt about 2 m. above Valenciennes.
ESCALA (LA), a town of Spain, in Catalonia, on the coast of the Mediterranean, in the prov. and 22 m. E of Gerona. Pop. 2,295.
ESCALANTE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 10 m. ESE of Santander.
ESCALAO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 16 m. N by W of Almeida.
ESCALO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Lerida, 25 m. WNW of Urgel.

ESCALONA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 28 m. NW of Toledo, on the Alberche, with the title of a duchy. It stands on an eminence in a fruitful district, and contains 4 suburbs, 4 churches, 2 convents, and a castle. Pop. 2,000.
ESCALVADA (Punta), a mountain and district of Brazil, in the gov. of Matto-Grosso, between the Paraguay on the W, and the São Lourenco on the E.

ESCALVADA (SERRA), an extensive mountainrange in Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, running NE from the Serra Santa - Martha, over an extent of about 90 m., and uniting with the Serra-Pyreneos. It forms part of the great Serra-dos-Vertentes, which separates the basins of the Tocantins and Parana.

ESCAMBIA, a county in the W of the state of Florida, U. S. Area 600 sq. m. Pop. 3,993. It is skirted on the E by a river of the same name, which rises in Alabama, and flows into Pensacola bay through several channels, which have a number of islands between them that are overflowed at high water. It is navigable for upwards of 100 m., and is lined with forests of valuable timber.
ESCAMILLA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 45 m. E of Guadalaxara. ESC
ESCAMPS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Coulange-la-Vineuse. Pop. 1,053.
ESCANAFFLES, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 18 m. N of Tournay, on the Scheldt. Pop. 2,003.
ESCANUELA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 16 m. NW of Jaen.

ESCAPE POINT, a cape on the W coast of the island of Revillagedo, in the N. Pacific ocean, in N lat. 55° 37': so called by Vancouver, from his landing here after having been attacked by savages in Traitors' cove.
ESCAPE RIVER, a river of North America, which runs into the Mississippi in N lat. 43° 35'.— Also a river of N. Australia which falls into the sea in S lat. 10° 58'.
ESCARBOTIN, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Ault, 10 m. W of Abbeville. Pop. 695.
ESCARICHE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 12 m. SSE of Guadalaxara, on the r. bank of the Tajunna.
ESCAROZ, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. ENE of Pampeluna, near the l. bank of the Salazar.
ESCARSO POINT, a cape on the N coast of the island of Mindoro, in N lat. 13° 27'.
ESCASSEFORT, a village of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Seydres. Pop. 749.
ESCATALENS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn-et-Garonne, cant. of Montech. Pop. 1,213.
ESCATRON, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 42 m. SE of Saragossa. Pop. 2,263.
ESCAUDAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Bouchain. Pop. 1,130. ESCAUDŒUVRES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Cambrai. Pop. 1,289.
ESCAUT. See SCHELDT.

ESCHACH, a river of Wurtemberg, in the Danube circle, an affluent of the Iller on the l. bank .- Also another stream in Wurtemberg, in the Schwarzwald circle, an affluent of the Neckar.-Also a village of Wurtemberg, in the Jaxt circle, 4 m. N of Schleichingen. Pop. 500.
ESCH-SUR-L'ALZETTE, a village of Belgium, in the prov. and 9 m. SW of Luxemburg, on the Alzette river. Pop. 1,919.
ESCHAU, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, 30 m. W of Wurzburg, on the Elsava river. Pop. 740.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, cant. of Geispolsheim. Pop. 1,265.
ESCHELBACH, a village of Baden, 4 m. NNW of Hilsbach. Pop. 987.
ESCHELBRONN, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine. Pop. 877.
ESCHEN, a village of the Tyrol, in the principality of Lichtenstein. Pop. 700.
ESCHENAU, a town of Wurtemberg, in the Neckar circle, 6 m. E of Weinsberg. Pop. 991.

ESCHENBACH, a town of Bavaria, in Upper Franconia, 17 m. SE of Bayreuth. Pop. 1,800.— Also a town in the circle of Middle Franconia, 9 m. SE of Anspach.—Also a parish and v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Lucerne, bail. and 2 m. from Hochdorf. Pop. 1,014.
ESCHENZ, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Thurgau, on the Unter-see, opposite Stein. Pop. 2,016.
ESCHERSHAUSEN, a town of Brunswick, 15 m. NW of Eimbeck, on the Lenne river. Pop. 1,070.
ESCHHOLZ BAY, an indentation of the W shore of Russian America, in the bay of Good Hope, near the Arctic circle, in W long. 161° 10'. Remarkable fossil remains have been discovered here.
ESCHIL-OLU, a village of Turkey in Europe, in Bulgaria, sanjak of Nicopolis, S of Osman-Basar.
ESCHLBERG, a village of Austria, in the prov. of Austria above the Ens, circle of the Mühl, to the W of Gramastätten.
ESCHMUNEIN. See ASHMUNEIN.
ESCHOLZMATT, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 22 m. SW of Lucerne, bail. and 4 m. from Entlibuch, at an alt. of 2,795 ft. above sea-level. Pop., Catholics, 3,681.
ESCHREF. See ASHRUFF.
ESCH-SUR-LA-SURE, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, 9 m. W of Diekirch, on the Sûre. Pop. 785.

ESCHWEGE, a walled town of Hesse-Cassel, capital of the circle and of the two bailiwicks of the same name, in the prov. of Lower Hesse, on the l. bank of the Werra, 25 m. ESE of Cassel. Pop. in 1843, 6,042, of whom 250 were Jews. It has four principal gates; the streets are handsome and spacious, and the houses generally well built. The principal buildings are the castle, an ancient edifice formerly a residence of the landgraves of Hesse- Rotenburg; the churches, of which there are four, all Calvinist; the town-house, the hospital, and the lazaretto. E. possesses extensive manufactories of cloth, flannel, and hosiery, soap, tobacco, and leather, and several dye-works. Its trade consists chiefly in flannel, spun wool, salted pork, and instrument strings. This town is of considerable antiquity, and contains the remains of a Benedictine abbey founded in the 10th cent. It was constituted a free imperial town in the 12th century. During the Thirty years' war it was to a great extent reduced to ruin.—The circle comprises an area of 153 sq. m., of which twothirds consist of arable land, and one-third is covered with wood. It is divided into 6 bailiwicks; and contains 3 towns and 69 villages. Pop. in 1840, 40,152. It is generally fertile, and produces grain of all kinds, fruit especially plums and cherries, and tobacco. It is calculated that 2,400 horses, 1,980 oxen, 8,780 cows, 35,500 sheep, 6,000 pigs, and 3,000 goats are annually reared within its limits. It has also mines of coal and gypsum.

ESCHWEILER, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, circle and 13 m. ENE of Aix-la-Chapelle, and on the railroad thence to Cologne, on the 1. bank of the Inde. Pop. in 1846, 7,884. It has manufactories of linen, damask, silk and woollen fabrics, ribbon, wax-cloth, iron-wire, needles, nails, and vitriol, and has also several dye-works and tanneries. Coal is wrought in the vicinity.
ESCLAVOS (Los), a town of Guatimala, 42 m. ESE of Guatimala.
ESCLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vosges, cant. of Darney. Pop. 1,450.
ESCOBAR-DE-CAMPOS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 34 m. SE of Leon, on the r. bank of the Sequillo.

ESCOCES (PUERTO), a small port of New Granada, in the SE part of Caroline bay, on the Atlantic coast of the isthmus, the site of the old Scotch settlement of New Edinburgh. It extends inward 3 m.; and has good anchorage in 44 to 7 fath. A scheme is now before the public for a ship canal between the Atlantic and Pacific, running from this point to Fuerte-del-Principe, a distance of 10 m.; thence down the river Savana 30 m. to its conflux with the Tuyra or Santa-Maria, just above the point where the latter discharges itself into the gulf of San Miguel. The rise of tide in the Savana, and for many miles up, is 21 ft. The distance from ocean to ocean is 40 m. ESC
ESCOCES (Punta), a headland on the coast of New Granada, forming the SE point of Caroline bay, and bearing 7 m. N 48° W from Point Caneto.
ESCOLATE, a small town of France, in Normandy, in the dep. of the Orne, on the Sarthe.
ESCOMBE, a chapelry in the p. and 12 m. WNW of Bishop-Auckland, in the co. of Durham. Area 840 acres. Pop. 510.
ESCONDIDA (PUERTO), a small inlet on the coast of New Granada, in the dep. of Chico, on the E coast of the bay of Panama, in 7° 37' N lat.

ESCONDIDO, a little port on the coast of Trinidad, N of Punta San Carlos.—There is a port of the same name on the coast of the prov. of Tabasco, in Mexico, at the entrance of Lake Terminos, and another on the south coast of the island of Cuba .- Also a creek on the E coast of Patagonia, 10 leagues from the river Negro, with a rise of tide varying from 24 to 32 ft.
ESCOPETE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 14 m. SE of Guadalaxara.
ESCORCA, a town of Majorca, 21 m. NNE of Palma. Excellent wine is made here.
ESCOT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenees, cant. of Accous. Pop. 820.
ESCOUAZA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. SW of Saint-Sebastian, on the l. bank of the Deva. Pop. 750.
ESCOUBLAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Guérande. Pop. 1,238.
ESCOULOUBRE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. of Roquefort-de- Sault. Pop. 749.
ESCOURCE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Sabres. Pop. 1,061.
ESCOUSSENS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn, cant. of La Bruguière. Pop. 1,211.
ESCOUTOUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Thiern. Pop. 2,121.
ESCRIVANOS (Point), a cape on the N coast of the isthmus of Darien. Gold has recently been found in one of the old Spanish workings of which there are many on this coast.
ESCRÆGNOLLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Saint-Vallier. Pop. 402.
ESCRICHE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 9 m. WNW of Teruel.
ESCUDO-DE-VERAGUAS, a small low island near the coast of the New Granada prov. of Veragua. -It is also the name of a river in the same prov., which forms the W limit of New Granada.

ESCUINTLA, a town of Guatimala, 35 m. SW of Guatimala city, on the Michatoyat river, in N lat. 14° 30'. Pop. 2,500, chiefly Indians.—The prov. of E., in the old arrangements of Guatimala, comprised the districts of E. and Guazacapan; and had an extent of 140 m. from NW to SE, with a breadth varying from 18 to 50 m. The principal streams in this district are the Michatoyat, the Rio-de-los-Esclavos, and the Guacalat.

ESCURIAL, or Escorial-de-Abajo, a town of Spain, containing 2,000 inhabitants, situated in a bare country, 25 m. NW of Madrid, on the SSE slope of the Sierra Guadarama, at an alt. of 2,970 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,326. It is celebrated for its palace, a magnificent structure erected by Philip II. in commemoration of the victory of St. Quentin, gained over the French in 1557. The building is properly a monastery dedicated to that saint; and in commemoration of the supposed manner of his death, the whole is cast into the form of a gridiron, -the towers, windows, altars, and other appendages. having all been placed with reference to this form. The palace, which is about 460 ft. in length, forms the handle of the gridiron; the rest consists of courts corresponding to the bars, and comprises 2 churches, and the cells of the monks, 200 in number. The whole structure is in the form of a parallelogram, and is 1,000 paces in circuit. It was the work of three different architects; and the total expense was nearly £3,000,000. The entrance to the palace on the W is through a beautiful portal of Doric pillars. The royal apartments contain some fine paintings arranged in two galleries. The library of the monastery possesses a valuable collection of manuscripts and old books, although part of the latter were destroyed by fire in 1671. The great church, which forms the most striking part of the building, is in the form of a Greek cross, with a lofty dome, and is 336 ft. long, and 230 ft. broad. Its roof and nave are adorned with beautiful paintings in fresco. Beneath this church is the pantheon, or place of interment for the royal family of Spain. Beautiful gardens adjoin the palace, but the surrounding country is dreary and uncultivated.
ESCUROLLES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Allier.—The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1841, 12,737.—The town is 4 m. NE of Gennat. Pop. 1,100.
ESCUVILLENS, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Friburg, 6 m. SSW of Friburg. Pop. 900.
ESENS, a town of Hanover, in the landr. of Aurich, on a river of the same name, near the German ocean, 20 m. NNE of Emden. Pop. 2,218.
ESFENDJAI, a town of Seistan, 60 m. SE of Dergasp.
ESFERE. See ASFURA.
ESFERLIK-BENASSI, a village of Servia, in the sanj. and 27 m. NE of Kruchovatz.
ESFILAGNA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 45 m. E of Grenada, on the r. bank of the Guadix.
ESGUEIRA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 8 m. S of Aveira. Pop. 2,900.
ESGUEVA, a river of Spain, which runs into the Pisuerga at Valladolid.
ESGUEVILLAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 26 m. E of Valladolid.

ESH, a chapelry in the p. of Lanchester, in Durham, 6 m. WNW of Durham. Area 2,910 acres. Pop. 518. There is a Roman Catholic chapel here; and an academical institution called Ushaw college, attended by about 120 students.

ESHER, a parish in Surrey, 131 m. NE of Guildford, on the road from London to Portsmouth, and intersected by the Northampton railway. Area 2,120 acres. Pop. 1,261. The v. is 13 m. distant by railway from London. Here are situated the mansions of Claremont and Esher-place, the former built for the celebrated Lord Clive, the conqueror of India, and purchased for Prince Leopold on his marriage with the Princess Charlotte of Wales, in 1816. Esher-place, a splendid Gothic structure, was occupied by Cardinal Wolsey when bishop of Winchester, and was also his retreat when disgraced by his capricious sovereign.
ESHOLT, a township in the p. of Orley, W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SW of Orley. Area 810 acres. Pop. 443.—Also a township in the p. of Felton, in Northumberland, 8 m. NW of Morpeth. Pop. 117.
ESHTON, a township in the p. of Gargreave, W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NW of Skipton. Pop. 74.
ESINO, a river of Italy, which, rising on the N slope of the Apennines, flows NW, and falls into the gulf of Venice between Ancona and Senegaglia, after a course of 40 m.

ESK, a river of Dumfries-shire and of Cumberland, formed by the confluent waters of the Black and the White E. From the point where these streams unite, the Esk flows 31 m. SSE through the p. of Westerkirk. Hemmed in here by Craighill, it sweeps N, and then assumes a direction due S, in the progress of \( \frac{3}{4} \) of a mile. It then flows E, N, and E, debouches to the S within the space of <math>1\frac{1}{2}</math> m., and thenceforth continues, with the exception of unimportant sinuosities, to have a direction to the E of S, till it receives the waters of the Liddal, and thence to the W of S, till it falls into the Solway frith. It flows past the town of Langholm, and there receives Ewes-water from the NE, and Wauchope-water from the SW. At Canonbie-holm it receives from the NE the wealthy tribute of the Liddal; and afterwards, for about 1 m., forms the boundary-line between Scotland and England. It then enters Cumberland, and having become an English river, receives from its fatherland the tribute of Glenzierburn, and from the land of its adoption the richer tribute of Line river, and, having flowed past Kirkandrews and Longtown, pours along toward the Solway frith at a point about 11 or 2 m. from Sarkfoot the extreme verge of Scotland. The Esk is a river of no common beauty, even in its upland regions. Over a great part of its entire course, it has a shelving or gravelly bottom, and glides along amidst lovely woodland scenery and smiling luxuriant haughs, which in former ages have oft re-echoed to the shouts of war. Measured from the confluence of the Black and the White E., its course, before leaving Scotland, is about 16 m.; and after entering Cumberland, between 7 and 8 m.
ESK, a small river of Cumberland, rising in the Sca-fell by several sources which unite near Eskdale. It flows past Underbank to Ravenglass, where it falls into an estuary common to it with the Mite:

ESK, a river and a lake in co. Donegal. The river issues from the lake, and has a run of only 3 m. SSW to the head of Donegal bay; and thence, or from the town of Donegal downward, it bears the name of the Donegal river. It is navigable from the Black rock at Durin to the bridge of Donegal .-- The lake covers only between 975 and 976 acres, yet is a beautiful sheet of water. The surface elevation of the lake above sea-level is 102 ft.

ESK (BLACK), a river of Dumfries-shire, rising in the mountain-range which forms the boundary of the co., and flowing S, cutting its way through a field of mountains, and receiving numerous tributary rills in its course, and eventually, after a course of 12 m., forming a confluence with the White Esk.

ESK (NORTH), a river of Forfarshire, formed by the confluent streams called East-water and Westwater, which rise amidst the mountain range of the Grampians, on the N boundary of the co. The Eastwater emerges from among the Grampians 4 m. above the point of confluence, and wears the character of strictly a Highland river. The West-water rises in Stoney-loch. In the upper and longer part of its course it resembles the East-water in being strictly a mountain-stream; and it flows altogether, in its independent course, about 22 m. The united waters pursue a direction somewhat sinuous, but in general E, traversing a distance of 9 m. to the German ocean, 3 m. N of Montrose. The entire course of the Esk, from the head-waters of the Mark, a head-stream of the East-water, is about 40 m.—There is another small river in Edinburghshire, which also bears the name of the North E. It rises in the p. of Linton in Peebles-shire, in the Easter-Cairn-hill, and flowing E and NE at the N limit of the p. of Dalkeith, unites with its sister-stream, the South E. The united waters of the Esks, denuded of their distinctive epithets of North and South, henceforth intersect the p. of Inveresk, cutting it into two nearly equal parts, and become ingulfed in the sea at the town of Musselburgh. The banks of the North Esk, over nearly its whole course, after entering Mid-Lothian, are delightfully picturesque and romantic. Amid all its beauty and its wealth of landscape, too, this river contributes largely to the useful and productive aims of agriculture and manufactory,—driving in its progress the machinery of numerous paper and other mills.

ESK (South), a river of considerable magnitude in Forfarshire. It rises in the extreme NW of the co., among the highest of the Grampian range, within a 1 m. of the source of a chief tributary of the Aberdeenshire Dee. It flows E and SE into Strathmore, and intersects the richest part of the coastdistrict which intervenes between that fine strath and the sea; sweeps past the town of Brechin, situated on its N bank; and suddenly expands into the beautiful lagune, 21 m. by 2 m., called Montrose basin: see Montrose. From this fine expanse—which alternately gleams in splendour under the flow of the tide, and during the recess of the waters darkens into the desolate aspect of a wide field of mud,—the river emerges by two narrow outlets, which fork round an island, and then open into a channel 4 m. wide, along which the river runs to embrace the ocean at the distance of <math>1\frac{1}{4}</math> m. from the exit from the basin. So narrow are the two gullets along the sides of the island, compared with the area and depth of the lagune, that the tide, both in entering and receding, moves with the impetuosity of a resistless current. Chiefly on this account, the South E. is of no benefit as a watery highway of communication further than <math>\frac{3}{4}</math> of a mile from the sea. This river in a former age produced pearls of great value.

ESK (South), a small river of Edinburghshire, the sister-stream of the North E. It issues, in the p. of Eddleston in Peebles-shire, from a small lake called West loch, and flows N, and then E; receives on its r. bank the tribute of Tweeddale-burn and Gladhouse-water; then flows NW, and N, till it blends its waters with those of the North E., after sweeping past the town and palace of Dalkeith, enclosing them between its own waters and those of its sister-stream in a long peninsula.

ESK (WHITE), a river of Dumfries-shire, of similar character to the Black E., and flowing parallel to it at an average distance of 3 m. to the E. Its sources are in the mountains 1 m. E of Ettrick-pen. The course of the river, till it forms a confluence with the Black E., is, including windings, about 15 or 16 m., and prevailingly S.

ESK, or Easky, a lake of co. Sligo, on the E base of the Lurgan hills. It is about 14 m. long, and gives birth to the river Easky. Its elevation above sea-level is 607 ft.; its area comprises 340 acres.

ESK, or Caha, the general name of the mountainrange which forms the backbone of the peninsula between Bantry-bay and the Kenmare estuary, and extends its summit-line between the Glengariff district of co. Cork, and the Killarney district of co. Kerry. The new road, passing direct from Glengariff to Killarney, and completed only two or three years ago, bears a close resemblance—though on a much reduced scale—to the grand road over the Simplon Alps. It winds among the escarpments and defiles of the range at a gradient of ascent sufficiently easy for general traffic; passes through three shoulders or abutments of mountain by tunnels of respectively 45, 84, and 600 ft. in length; and finally approaches Kenmare by a suspension-bridge of 410 ft. across the Kenmare river.

ESKDALE, the E district of Dumfries-shire, and the smallest of the three dales or sections into which that co. is popularly divided. These sections seem never to have had fixed or accurately defined boundaries; and are loosely represented as corresponding with the watersheds of the great rivers, the Nith, ESK the Annan, and the Esk, by which they are respectively traversed. Excepting the p. of Canonbie, and a stripe of the southern part of that of Langholm, all Eskdale is hilly or mountainous, constituting a large part of the Southern Highlands of Scotland, and presenting a bleak and rugged aspect, relieved at intervals by glimpses of beauty.
ESKDALE AND WASDALE, a chapelry in the p. of St. Bees, Cumberland, 7 m. NE by E of Ravenglass. Pop. in 1801, 232; in 1841, 375.

ESKDALESIDE, a township and chapelry in the p. of Whitby, N. R. of Yorkshire, 53 m. SW by W of Whitby, intersected by the Pickering and Whitby railway. Area 4,150 acres. Pop. in 1801, 344; in 1841, 519.

ESKE, a river of Yorkshire, which rises by many sources, in the centre of the eastern Moorland dales, and flowing E, is joined by various other streams, until, after a course of about 25 m., it falls into the North sea at Whitby, which it divides into two nearly equal parts, connected by a drawbridge.
ESKER. See ISKER.
ESKERDU, a village of Little Tibet, 95 m. NW of Ladak, in N lat. 30° 56'. *** ESKI, a Turkish adjunct signifying 'old;' and of frequent use in eastern nomenclature.
ESKI-ANTALIYAH. See SIDE.
ESKI-BABA, a small town of Turkey, in Romelia, in the sanj. of Kirkkilissia, on the route from Hafsa to Chourli.
ESKI-CRIM. See CRIM.
ESKI-EREGLI. See EREGLI.

ESKI-HISSAR, a village of Asiatic Turkey, 16 m. W of Moglah, built upon wooded and rising grounds, and environed with high branches of the Taurus. Tobacco is grown on the neighbouring plain. This city is supposed to stand on the site of the ancient Stratonicea, founded by a colony of Macedonians; and there still exist here the ruins of a theatre, and some scattered pilasters, columns, inscriptions, and sepulchres .-- Also a village in the sanj. of Katahiyah, 7 m. N of Degnizli.
ESKI-JALSA, a town of Russian Armenia, 120 m. SE of Erivan.
ESKI-JUMA, a village of Turkey, in Bulgaria, in the sanj. of Rustchuk, 18 m. W of Shumla.

ESKILSTUNA, a town of Sweden, in the laen of Nyköping, situated near the lake of Hjelmar, on the Hjelmar-A, 60 m. NNW of Nyköping. Its inhabitants, amounting to 2,486 in 1833, are chiefly employed in iron and steel manufactures.

ESKI-SAGRA, or Eski-Zagara, a town of European Turkey, 70 m. NW of Adrianople, on the S flank of the Balkan, in a highly cultivated country, watered by an affluent of the Tundsha. It has 8 mosques. The pop., said to amount to 20,000, are employed in various manufactures, the chief of which are leather, carpets, and hardware.—D'Anville supposes that this place may mark the site of the ancient Beræa, which was situated on the confines of Thrace Proper, and re-established by the empress Irene.

ESKI-SHEHR, a town of Anatolia, 27 m. ENE of Kutaiah, on the site of the ancient Dorylaium. It stands at the foot of a range of hills bounding to the S a plain about 14 m. in length, a little to the W of the junction of the Poursac or Thymbrius with a smaller stream. Not far from the Poursac are mineral baths, in high repute among the Turks.

ESKI-STAMBUL, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the sanj. of Biga, 5 m. SE of the island of Tenedos. It is built upon a headland jutting out into the sea 5½ leagues N of Cape Baba, in N lat. 39° 45′, E long. 26° 10'. The adjacent country is thickly covered with valonia oaks and other large timber; and the arsenals of Constantinople fetch much of their in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, on the Douro, and supplies from this port. In the vicinity are the ruins of the ancient Alexandria Troas; and the ancient hotbaths are still found here.

ESLA, a river of Spain, which rises in the mountains of Asturias, NW of Morana, and falls into the Douro, between Miranda-de-Douro and Zamora, after a course of 150 m. from NNE to SSW. Its principal affluents are the Cea on the l.; and the Curuenno, Torio, and Orvigo on the r.
ESLARN, a town of Bavaria, in the Upper Pfalz, 35 m. ENE of Amberg.
